metadata.title: ZenMux Knowledge Quiz Bank
metadata.description: Knowledge quiz questions used during the ZenMux subscription purchase flow. Community contributions are welcome. Question types include single-choice and true/false.

questions.question: What does CC stand for in today's AI context?
questions.explanation: In today's AI development context, CC specifically refers to Claude Code — the AI coding assistant CLI tool developed by Anthropic. It runs directly in the terminal and supports tasks such as code generation, refactoring, and debugging.

questions.question: When you launch Claude Code on a medium-sized project and send 'hello', roughly how many tokens are consumed and how many API requests are made?
questions.explanation: When Claude Code starts up, it automatically reads the project directory structure, CLAUDE.md, and relevant source files to build context. Even sending just 'hello' triggers multiple background API requests (e.g., file indexing, context construction), resulting in total token consumption typically in the thousands to tens of thousands, with request counts generally between 3 and 10. This is one of the key differences between Claude Code and calling the API directly.

questions.question: Sending the same 'hello' message to Opus 4.6 and Sonnet 4.6 consumes the same number of ZenMux Flows.
questions.explanation: ZenMux Flows is a unified billing unit, but different models have different per-token prices. Opus 4.6 has higher input/output token rates than Sonnet 4.6, so even sending identical content consumes a different number of Flows. The more capable the model, the higher its price and the more Flows it uses.

questions.question: In the ZenMux Builder Plan subscription, one Flow equals one API request.
questions.explanation: A Flow is the billing unit for ZenMux subscriptions — it is not equivalent to a request count. Think of Flows as a currency: there is a floating exchange rate between Flows and USD (currently 1 Flow ≈ $0.03283). How many Flows a single request consumes depends on the model used and the token volume: more capable models charge more per token, so the same message will consume more Flows.

questions.question: Which of the following accurately describes ZenMux's two billing methods?

options.text: Both Flows and Pay As You Go Credits are fixed at 1 USD each
Flows have a floating exchange rate against USD; Pay As You Go Credits are fixed at 1 Credit = $1
Pay As You Go Credits have a floating value; Flows are fixed at 1 Flow = $1
Both are billed per API request, with no relation to token consumption

questions.explanation: ZenMux has two billing systems. The subscription plan uses Flows, which have a floating exchange rate against USD (currently ~1 Flow = $0.03283); this rate is adjusted periodically in response to market conditions and model pricing. Pay As You Go uses Credits, where 1 Credit = $1 exactly, with charges based on actual token consumption. The two systems suit different use cases: the subscription plan is ideal for individual development and vibe coding, while PAYG is designed for production environments.

questions.question: Which of the following scenarios is the ZenMux Builder Plan subscription designed for?

options.text: A live, monetized SaaS product serving end users
Personal vibe coding, learning, and rapid prototyping
High-concurrency API integration in an enterprise production environment
Rotating multiple accounts to bypass rate limits

questions.explanation: The Builder Plan subscription is designed for personal development, learning, vibe coding, and other non-production use cases. Once a project goes live or becomes commercial, you must switch to Pay As You Go. Options A and C represent production environments; option D (pooling multiple accounts to bypass rate limits) violates the Terms of Service. All three are prohibited under the subscription plan.

questions.question: Approximately what is the rate limit (RPM) when calling the API with a ZenMux Builder Plan subscription key?

options.text: Unlimited — no cap on requests per minute

questions.explanation: The Builder Plan subscription enforces a rate limit of approximately 10–15 requests per minute (RPM), along with a rolling 5-hour quota window and a weekly usage cap. This is one of the core reasons the subscription plan is not suitable for production environments. If you need high-concurrency, rate-limit-free API access, use the Pay As You Go plan instead.

questions.question: The ZenMux Builder Plan subscription API key and the Pay As You Go API key are the same and can be used interchangeably.
questions.explanation: The two API keys are completely separate and cannot be used interchangeably. PAYG keys begin with `sk-ai-`, while subscription keys begin with `sk-ss-v1-`. Using the wrong key will result in failed calls or billing errors — make sure to use the correct key for each plan.

questions.question: Which types of AI models does the ZenMux platform currently support (applicable to both the subscription plan and PAYG)?
questions.explanation: ZenMux covers a broad range of AI model types: text generation models (Claude, GPT-5, Gemini, etc., for chat and coding), image generation models (NanoBananaPro, GPT-Image-1.5, etc.), and video generation models — delivering true all-in-one coverage across Coding, Image, Video, and Chat with a single API key.

questions.question: For both PAYG top-ups and subscriptions on ZenMux, how long after purchase must a refund request be submitted?
questions.explanation: Under Section IX of the ZenMux Terms of Service, refund requests for both PAYG top-ups and subscriptions must be submitted within 24 hours of purchase. Requests submitted after this window will not be honored. Additionally, a 5% platform service fee is deducted from the refund amount. To apply, email support@zenmux.ai with your account details, purchase time, and reason for the refund.

questions.question: According to the ZenMux Privacy Policy, in which region is user-uploaded data (including personal information and content) typically stored?
questions.explanation: Under Section II of the ZenMux Privacy Policy, all data uploaded or transmitted by users — including personal information and user content — is typically stored in Singapore. ZenMux's operating entity, AI Force Singapore Pte. Ltd., is incorporated in Singapore, operates under Singapore law, and disputes are subject to the exclusive jurisdiction of Singapore courts.

questions.question: What percentage of the refund amount does ZenMux deduct as a platform service fee when processing a refund?
questions.explanation: Under Section 9.2.1 of the ZenMux Terms of Service, a 5% platform service fee is deducted from all refunds. The calculation is as follows — PAYG refund: unused top-up credit value × 95%; subscription refund: (total subscription fee paid − amount already consumed) × 95%. Refunds must be requested within 24 hours of purchase, and once approved, funds take 3–10 business days to arrive.

questions.question: When a ZenMux Builder Plan subscription reaches its 5-hour or weekly quota limit, what does the Extra Usage (automatic overage switching) feature do?

options.text: Immediately interrupts service — you must wait until the quota resets to continue
Automatically switches to the linked Pay As You Go API Key so usage continues uninterrupted
Queues subsequent requests and retries them in batch once the quota resets
Automatically upgrades the user's subscription to a higher tier

questions.explanation: Extra Usage is the Builder Plan's automatic overage switching mechanism. When subscription usage hits the 5-hour or weekly quota limit, ZenMux automatically switches to the Pay As You Go API Key pre-linked on the Subscription Management page, billing against your PAYG balance so your dev/coding/chat workflow isn't interrupted. When the subscription quota window resets, it switches back to the subscription key automatically.

questions.question: Once Extra Usage is enabled, the system automatically switches back to subscription billing as soon as the subscription quota window resets — no manual action required.
questions.explanation: Automatic recovery is a core behavior of Extra Usage: when the subscription quota is exhausted it switches to the PAYG key, and as soon as the 5-hour or weekly window resets it switches back to subscription billing. The whole process is transparent to the client — you keep using the same subscription key and never need to swap API keys in your IDE or terminal.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes how Extra Usage is configured?

options.text: You can only link a newly created PAYG API Key — existing keys are not supported
On the Subscription Management page you can pick an existing PAYG API Key from the dropdown, or click Create new key to create one
Once configured it cannot be changed unless you cancel your subscription
You must email support to enable it — it cannot be self-configured in the console

questions.explanation: On the Subscription Management page, toggling Extra Usage opens a "Select a Key" dialog where you can choose an existing PAYG API Key from the dropdown, or click "Create new key" to create one on the fly. After setup, you can click "Reselect" anytime to change the backup key, or flip the toggle off to disable the feature — everything is fully self-service.

questions.question: After Extra Usage switches over to the PAYG key, how are calls made during the overage period billed?

options.text: They continue to consume the subscription's Flows quota
They are billed as Pay As You Go Credits against actual token consumption, deducted from the PAYG balance
They are free and incur no charges
They are recorded as debt and deducted together in the next subscription cycle

questions.explanation: Once Extra Usage triggers a switch, billing shifts from Flows to PAYG: charges are deducted from the Pay As You Go balance as Credits based on actual token consumption (1 Credit = $1). Before relying on the feature, make sure your PAYG account has enough balance — otherwise calls will still fail after the switch. It's also a good idea to monitor Extra Usage spending on the Subscription Management page to avoid unexpected costs.

questions.question: After enabling Extra Usage, users must manually swap the API key in their client from the subscription key to the PAYG key, and then swap it back when the quota resets.
questions.explanation: The key selling point of Extra Usage is seamless, automatic switching. The client continues to use the original subscription key (starting with `sk-ss-v1-`); when the quota is exhausted, the ZenMux backend silently switches billing to the linked PAYG key (starting with `sk-ai-`), and switches back when the quota resets. The entire process is transparent to your dev/coding/chat workflow — no manual key changes are required.

questions.question: Which scenario is Extra Usage best suited for?

options.text: A high-concurrency production environment that needs to bypass subscription rate limits entirely
Cases where the subscription quota is generally enough, but occasionally runs out near the end of the 5-hour or weekly window, interrupting ongoing dev/coding work
Beginner users who don't want to set up PAYG and only want to use the subscription plan
Sharing a single subscription quota across multiple accounts

questions.explanation: Extra Usage is designed to solve the specific pain point of "my subscription quota is normally enough, but occasionally runs out at the end of a window and interrupts my work." It's not a fit for A (high-concurrency production workloads should use PAYG directly for unrestricted RPM), C (the feature requires an active PAYG balance to function), or D (pooling a subscription across multiple accounts violates the Terms of Service).
